rawDescription: Given an array, I needed to find the number of subarrays whose sum is divisible by k. It required using prefix sums and modulo arithmetic with a hash map.

problem.title: Subarray Sums Divisible by K
problem.description: Given an integer array `nums` and an integer `k`, return the total number of continuous subarrays whose sum is divisible by `k`.

visibleTestCases.explanation: There are 7 subarrays with a sum divisible by k = 5: [4, 5, 0, -2, -3, 1], [5], [5, 0], [0], [0, -2, -3], [-2, -3], [1]

problem.editorial: Maintain a running prefix sum and compute its remainder modulo k. Track the frequency of each remainder using a hash map. If a remainder has been seen before, it means the subarray between the two positions has a sum divisible by k. Be sure to handle negative remainders by converting them to equivalent positive remainders.

rawDescription: Classic Levenshtein distance problem using dynamic programming to find the minimum operations to convert one word to another.

problem.title: Edit Distance
problem.description: Given two strings `word1` and `word2`, return the minimum number of operations required to convert `word1` to `word2`. You have the following three operations permitted on a word: Insert a character, Delete a character, Replace a character.

visibleTestCases.explanation: horse -> rorse (replace 'h' with 'r'), rorse -> rose (remove 'r'), rose -> ros (remove 'e')

problem.editorial: Define a 2D table `dp[i][j]` representing the edit distance between prefixes `word1[0...i-1]` and `word2[0...j-1]`. If `word1[i-1] == word2[j-1]`, then `dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]`. Otherwise, `dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1])` for deletion, insertion, and replacement operations respectively.

rawDescription: Given a dictionary, find the shortest transformation sequence from a begin word to an end word. Solved it using BFS level-by-level queue optimization.

problem.title: Word Ladder
problem.description: A transformation sequence from word `beginWord` to word `endWord` using a dictionary `wordList` is a sequence of words `beginWord -> s1 -> s2 -> ... -> sk` such that every adjacent pair of words differs by a single letter and all words are in `wordList`. Return the number of words in the shortest transformation sequence, or `0` if no such sequence exists.

visibleTestCases.explanation: One shortest transformation sequence is "hit" -> "hot" -> "dot" -> "dog" -> "cog", which is 5 words long.

problem.editorial: Treat the words as nodes in an unweighted graph where an edge exists if two words differ by exactly one character. Use Breadth-First Search (BFS) to traverse outward from `beginWord`. To optimize finding neighbors, replace each character of the current word from 'a' to 'z' and check membership in the word set.

rawDescription: Asked if a string can become a palindrome if you are allowed to delete at most one character. Used two pointers.

problem.title: Valid Palindrome II
problem.description: Given a string `s`, return `true` if the `s` can be palindrome after deleting at most one character from it.
problem.editorial: Use two pointers, one at the start and one at the end of the string, moving inward. If the characters match, continue. If they mismatch, check if the remaining substring becomes a palindrome by either skipping the left character or skipping the right character.

rawDescription: Sliding window problem where you find the longest substring containing the same letter after replacing at most k characters.

problem.title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
problem.description: You are given a string `s` and an integer `k`. You can choose any character of the string and change it to any other uppercase English character. You can perform this operation at most `k` times. Return the length of the longest substring containing the same letter you can get after performing the above operations.

visibleTestCases.explanation: Replace the two 'A's with two 'B's or vice versa.

problem.editorial: Use a sliding window. Maintain a frequency map of characters inside the window and keep track of the count of the most frequent character. If the total window size minus the max frequency character count exceeds `k`, the window is invalid, so shrink it from the left.

rawDescription: Find all paths in a binary tree that sum to a target value. Paths don't need to start at the root or end at a leaf.

problem.title: Path Sum III
problem.description: Given the `root` of a binary tree and an integer `targetSum`, return the number of paths where the sum of the values along the path equals `targetSum`. The path does not need to start or end at the root or a leaf, but it must go downwards.

visibleTestCases.explanation: The paths that sum to 8 are: [5, 3], [5, 2, 1], [-3, 11]

problem.editorial: Combine DFS traversal with a prefix sum technique similar to the 'Subarray Sum Equals K' problem. Use a hash map to store the frequencies of prefix sums running from the root down to the current node. When backtracking, remove the current prefix sum value from the map.

rawDescription: 0-1 Knapsack problem variations where you determine if an array can be partitioned into two subsets with equal sums.

problem.title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
problem.description: Given an integer array `nums`, return `true` if you can partition the array into two subsets such that the sum of the elements in both subsets is equal or `false` otherwise.

visibleTestCases.explanation: The array can be partitioned as [1, 5, 5] and [11].

problem.editorial: First calculate the sum of the total array. If it is odd, return false immediately. If it is even, the target sum for a subset is `sum / 2`. Turn it into a subset sum problem using dynamic programming to track if a target sum can be formed using a combination of array values.

rawDescription: Graph question asking to count the number of separate components using Union-Find or DFS matrix tracking.

problem.title: Number of Connected Components in an Undirected Graph
problem.description: You have a graph of `n` nodes. You are given an integer `n` and an array `edges` where `edges[i] = [ai, bi]` indicates that there is an edge between `ai` and `bi` in the graph. Return the number of connected components in the graph.
problem.editorial: Initialize the count of components to `n`. Iterate through each edge and join the components using a Disjoint Set Union (DSU / Union-Find) structure. Every time a union operation successfully merges two previously disconnected nodes, decrement the total component count by one.

rawDescription: Given an array of numbers, mutate it to the next lexicographically greater permutation using pointers and standard sorting steps.

problem.title: Next Permutation
problem.description: A permutation of an array of integers is an arrangement of its members into a sequence or linear order. Find the next lexicographically greater permutation of its integer elements. If such arrangement is not possible, the array must be rearranged as the lowest possible order (i.e., sorted in ascending order). The replacement must be in place.
problem.editorial: Find the first pair of elements from the right where `nums[i] < nums[i+1]`. Let this index be the breakpoint. Next, find the smallest element to the right of the breakpoint that is strictly greater than `nums[breakpoint]`. Swap them, then reverse the entire suffix sequence to the right of the breakpoint.

rawDescription: Modify standard level order traversal so that levels cycle back and forth dynamically from left-to-right then right-to-left.

problem.title: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
problem.description: Given the `root` of a binary tree, return the zigzag level order traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, then right to left for the next level and alternate between).

visibleTestCases.explanation: Level 0 goes left-to-right [3]. Level 1 goes right-to-left [20,9]. Level 2 goes left-to-right [15,7].

problem.editorial: Utilize a standard queue structure for level-order processing. Introduce a boolean flag to track whether the current level should be appended in normal order or reversed order. When storing node values into the intermediate current level list, insert at the front or the back depending on the value of the flag.
